sentences.text: Los Angeles.
My name is Mike Popik.
I was born in May 11, 1931, Levice, Czechoslovakia.
Levice is about 100 kilometer from Bratislava, close to the Hungarian border.
P-O-P-I-K.
Miki.
Well, at home, I just spelled it M-I-K-I.
My father's name is William Popik, and my mother's name was Frida Popik [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah, I had a brother, Andras Popik.
He was 16 and 1/2.
And my younger brother, Gabi, 2 and 1/2 years old.
Well, it was a small city in Slovakia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably 18,000 or 19,000 people and 2,000 Jews.
It was a nice city, and we had a fairly good life there.
Sure.
I was in school.
Just elementary.
It was-- well, I finished.
It was actually a Hungarian school.
Because as you know, in 1938, this part of Slovakia was given to the Hungarians.
So I finished few grades in Slovakian schools and five grades-- yeah, five grades in the Hungarian schools.
So I finished seven, almost eight grades.
Well, not Hebrew.
Well, we had the Jewish education once a week when our Gentile friends had their religion.
Ours, we went to a Jewish school just for-- I think, for two or three hours a week.
That's all.
Yes, we were observant.
Not too religious, but observant.
Shabbas was Shabbas, and holidays were holidays.
And we observed all the holidays.
We kept kosher till 1941.
After '41, it was impossible to keep kosher.
Well, my father was a partner in a truck-- in a trucking company with two Gentile partners.
The three owners, they had five trucks, and they were also mechanics and the drivers.
So we did all right.
Mother was a housewife.
Spoke in Hungarian and Slovak.
And some German.
Some German.
No Yiddish.
Sure, of course.
Very good.
We were only two, maybe three Jewish families in the entire street where we lived.
And we had very good relationships with the Gentile population.
Non-Jews.
Yes, very well.
Yes, some of them were very good friends, yes.
Yes, only doing the Hungarian occupation.
Not in the Slovak or Czechoslovakian occupation.
Well, what I remember is that one day, my mother and my father told us that the Hungarians are occupying our city.
And my mother was born in Budapest.
So she was waiting for the Hungarians.
And in the morning, the day the Czechoslovakian army moved out from our city, and they throw garbage all over with their tanks to disturb the Hungarian army to parade into our city, Levice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- we liked the Czechoslovakian army.
We were-- we couldn't believe that they are moving out.
But of course, as a little boy, so we waiting it for some-- for the changes.
So the Hungarians came in.
It was-- I think it was November-- end of November 1938.
I was seven years old.
And when the Hungarian army came into the town, it was a terrible disappointment.
They looked differently than the-- than the Czechoslovakian army.
They were very poorly dressed.
But the main thing is they were singing anti-Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] slogans and anti-Jewish songs.
So it was a terrible disappointment to the Jewish population.
Yes, of course, because we spoke Hungarian.
Just dirty Jews, you are going to Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we couldn't believe it.
It was a drastic change.
At night, a bus came to our city, a huge bus.
I remember the bus because I never saw such a big bus before.
Those were the Hungarian-- the representatives of the Hungarian government.
And they went to lay flowers on the tomb of the unknown soldier.
So we are children.
We ran after the car.
We wanted to see, with my Gentile friends and Jewish friends.
And at the tomb of the unknown soldier, they were yelling those anti-Jewish slogans.
So I just left my friends, and I ran home.
And I was crying.
And I told my father, my mother what did they-- what I heard.
So the father tried to quiet the things down, that it's-- it's just some people that it doesn't have too much importance.
And it really, the things went OK for the first couple months.
But the difference was tremendous after the Czechoslovakians left.
I wanted to ask you, do you think your father told you it wouldn't make a big difference because he wanted to comfort you--
Of course.
--a child that was--
Of course.
--you know, obviously--
Of course, yes.
No, he was just trying to comfort us.
He saw the-- the disaster.
Yeah.
Well, we know very little.
The truth is, we know very little.
Because from the beginning, the first couple years were fairly OK.
But in 1941, when they started the anti-Jewish laws, then our radios were taken away, and the news was pro-German, although the Hungarians directly did not attack the Jews.
But the-- the children couldn't go to the gymnasiums.
The-- all the-- the children couldn't go to study doctors and lawyers.
So slowly, slowly, we adopted to this different and antisemitic life.
And the Gentile population, they adapted also.
Slowly, slowly they became anti-- antisemites.
Most of them, if not all of them, but most of them.
So they adapted in a negative way?
Negative way.
The teachers especially.
Very few teachers were that they had-- they were passionate to us and with feelings.
But most of the teachers were antisemites during the Hungarian rule.
No, it was an opportunity.
This is my feeling.
My feeling, and then after war when I visited there, I asked my friends.
And they said that it was because of the opportunity.
Yes, benefit till we were there, they were benefiting for-- for their jobs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No question about it.
And also, slowly, slowly, they pushed out the Jews from their positions.
So business wise, they take over the-- slowly, slowly, the businesses.
They took over the factories.
Very slowly.
Doctors.
There were no Jewish children graduated as doctors.
So they had the most opportunity-- sure, opportunity.
Excellent memory, yeah.
I remember everything.
I remember all the details.
Yeah.
Before was very pleasant, playing soccer, like all the Slovak or Hungarian children, playing with our Gentile friends.
Going to-- when I was very little, I didn't have too much opportunities, but we-- we had a normal life.
Normal life.
Very little.
We traveled.
I traveled and our family traveled because we had-- we had cars.
But to travel with a train, if you traveled 20 kilometers, this was a big travel already. 20, up to 30 kilometers, it was a big travel.
This was the travel for us.
Yes, sure.
Sure, I belonged to Maccabi.
To the-- but Maccabi, it was a sport club.
It had nothing to do with Israel.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.
Activities, going fishing, camping, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nice-- nice prewar European life.
Yes, we were interested.
But I think the news were traveling very slow.

Most of them, most of the things we did not believe what is happening, because we just couldn't believe that those things that the Polish children-- they ran away from some camps, concentration camps, and ended up in our city, two of them, in 1942.
They told us horror stories that even the rabbi, Saturday in the shul, told us that we shouldn't believe.
Those are gypsy children, and the stories are just not true.
No, those were Jewish-Polish children.
But for some reason, the Hungarians and the Polish, they didn't like each other.
For some reason, they called them gypsies.
This is what I remember.
Yes, those children, I went to-- to school together with them.
And it was 1942 when those two children arrived in our city.
And one Jewish family without children took them in.
And they were-- they went to school with us.

But when they arrived, they told us, when they already learned the Hungarian language, they told us that their family, the entire [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish population from their town, was herded to a synagogue, and they were burned alive.
So it was very hard to believe.
But if I remember, they were crying when they told us those stories.
So we didn't believe them.
I can't remember their names.
They were given Hungarian names.
Their original names, I don't remember.
And we-- we just went to the same school.
They were-- they were older than I was.
We went to the same school, so I remember, they went to school with us, and they went back to Auschwitz with us and probably didn't even survive.
When they arrived, they were children of 14, probably 14, 15 years old.
Don't remember.
From Poland, they came.
They spoke Yiddish.
Yiddish and probably-- and we-- we did not spoke Yiddish in our town.
So communicate them from the beginning was very hard.
But they learned the language very fast, and we played together with them.
No, 2,000 together.
Yes.
Most of them were observant Jews.
How did the town?
First of all, it was-- it was-- as I remember, it was a center of a community.
It was a main city from surrounding small towns.
So agriculture was booming, and some factories.
As a matter of fact, all the factories I remember were-- belonged to Jews.
I remember it was a sugar factory, a huge sugar factory.
Like candies, candy factory, textile factory.
Yes.
The-- the-- the candy factory belonged to Kleins, family Klein.
Then the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the building supply, huge building supply, belonged to-- the Roth family, very rich.
Schultz family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Deutch family.
They were all-- they had factories.
They had factories.
Small, but very well doing.

Well, they had different-- they had all-- they were doctors and dental technicians and lawyers and-- and businesses and profession, mechanics and photographers, and everything that you can find between the-- the Gentile population, we had it.
Yes, I remember.
Rabbi-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I-- now I can't remember.
But I-- I know his name.
I just can't remember.
I can see his face, even.
I don't remember his name now.
Yeah, maybe later on.
I don't know what was the real reason.
But I have a feeling, especially after the war, that all the rabbis were, all over, was almost the same story.
They tried to minimize the-- the problems.
They were probably told by the government officials to keep cool, to-- not to panic, to not to try and run away, to not to make stupid things.
They did it very smart.
Not the rabbis, but the people that give them the orders.
And this is what the rabbis did everywhere.
No.
Minimize, yes.
Minimize.
Maybe they knew much-- much more than we did, although I don't believe it.
I don't know.
I don't know.
I saw only one-- I remember only the one rabbi from our temple.
We had a beautiful, big temple there.
And the rabbi and the cantor, they were deported, just like we were.
We tried to stay together and to believe that everything will be OK.
Probably it was then too late.
I-- this I don't know.
But when we were taken, and they concentrated us in an army complex-- this was actually the-- not the ghetto.
In the ghetto, those were designated streets for the Jewish population from the beginning, because Jews from the entire area came to Levice.
We were the big city.
And so they were streets designated only for Jews.
From the ghetto-- that we were only one month in the ghetto-- we were taken to this huge army complex.
And we were about over 2,000 there.
So it was already very hard to communicate with people.
I mean, we didn't know where are the people.
And he had no leaders between us.
So it was hard to know what was their thinking.
But everybody said that we will be relocated deep in Hungaria in some work camps.
And we believed till the last minute.
The Hungarians.
The Hungarians and the Jews, that they are going to relocate us in working-- work camps.
In Levice, in May 1944.
No, just isolated streets.
And between the-- between the-- the-- the front and the beginning of the street and end of the street was just barbed wire.
And some Hungarian soldiers were watching it.
And the people that were watching it were not really dressed as soldiers.
They [? wore ?] civilian clothing.
They had, I think, the Hungarian banner on that the Hungarian had, something like this.
No, not the Arrow Cross.
In our city was no-- no functioning Arrow Cross.
They were people belong to the Arrow Cross, and they were shouting this, dirty Jews.
But was no official-- I don't remember that they were parading like the Arrow Cross.
We couldn't leave those-- those streets.
No, of course not.
No, of course not.
Well, the war came to me that, in 1938, the Czechoslovakian Army was mobilizing.
My father was a sergeant in the Czechoslovakian Army, and he was mobilized.
And I was very proud of him when I saw him all dressed up as a soldier.
And we were-- they were taken to the train station.
Everything moved by trains then.
So I was happy, but I was also crying that the father left.
And they went somewhere.
I can't remember where they-- where they took them exactly because when he wrote to us, he had only army numbers, no addresses.
So when the Hungarians [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took over Levice, he was in the Czechoslovakian Army.
So we were terrible concerned that the father is in the Czechoslovakian Army, and we are already in Hungaria.
But three or four months later, we get orders that-- I mean, news that older parents-- I mean, fathers and children, that they left the Czechoslovakian Army.
They are coming back, walking back from Czechoslovakia, and they are coming home.
So every day, we ran to the Slovakian border, waiting for them.
Me, my brother, and the friends.
We were running, waiting for them, because we were told that they are walking home.
They didn't give them transportation.
But one day they really arrived.
And we were happy.
Disappointed, but happy that they came home.
And they came home, you know, very dirty looking.
And because they were walking for so long, probably weeks, from somewhere in Slovakia.
And they were-- even though my father was Slovakian, but they-- they didn't give them help in Slovakia because they were considered Hungarians.
So.
But they came home.
And my father, because he was a diver, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if he was privileged, but he was driving even after, driving his trucks between Slovakia and Hungaria.
So this way it started for me, the war.
But we got used to it, and everything was OK.
Even we got used to that-- that our friends are calling us dirty Jew.
You know, day by day, it was tough.
But the real war started for me when the Germans walked into my town.
And this was in April, Pesach 1944.
